title: How Coral Reefs Support Marine Ecosystems
passage:
Coral reefs are often called the rainforests of the sea because they support a large variety of life in a relatively small area. Tiny coral animals build hard structures that create hiding places, feeding zones, and breeding grounds for fish, crustaceans, and many other organisms. Even species that do not live on the reef permanently may depend on it for shelter during early stages of life.

Reefs also help keep nearby waters productive. By slowing waves and currents, they create calmer spaces where small plants and plankton can grow. Those organisms become food for larger animals, which means the reef supports a chain of life that extends beyond its own surface. In healthy reef systems, this network can make the surrounding ecosystem more resilient after storms or seasonal changes.

The passage of time, however, can damage reefs when water becomes too warm or polluted. When coral weakens, the animals that rely on it often lose shelter and food sources. For that reason, scientists study reef health not only to protect one habitat but also to understand the wider coastal system. The author mentions seagrass beds and mangrove forests in the final paragraph to show that coral reefs work together with other habitats to support marine life and protect shorelines.

questions.question: What is the passage mainly about?

options.text: The many ways coral reefs support marine ecosystems
The history of reef-based tourism around the world
The causes of ocean storms near tropical islands
The process of building artificial coral structures

questions.evidence: The passage explains how reefs provide shelter, feeding areas, calmer waters, and wider ecosystem support.
questions.explanation: The central focus is the ecological role of coral reefs, not tourism, storms, or artificial construction.

questions.question: According to the passage, why do some young animals depend on reefs?

options.text: Reefs make the water too warm for predators
Reefs provide shelter during early stages of life
Reefs force animals to migrate farther offshore
Reefs contain fresh water for drinking

questions.evidence: Even species that do not live on the reef permanently may depend on it for shelter during early stages of life.
questions.explanation: The passage states directly that reefs offer shelter while young animals are still developing.

questions.question: In the passage, the word resilient most nearly means

options.text: easy to find
unusually bright
able to recover after change or damage
separated from other habitats

questions.evidence: The text says the network can make the surrounding ecosystem more resilient after storms or seasonal changes.
questions.explanation: The context shows that resilient means able to bounce back or remain strong after disturbance.

questions.question: What can be inferred about reefs when the coral weakens?

options.text: The surrounding ecosystem becomes more stable immediately
Animals that depend on the reef may lose shelter and food
Nearby waters always become colder
The reef will naturally replace mangrove forests

questions.evidence: When coral weakens, the animals that rely on it often lose shelter and food sources.
questions.explanation: The passage connects weak coral with reduced protection and fewer resources for reef-dependent animals.

questions.question: Why does the author mention seagrass beds and mangrove forests in the final paragraph?

options.text: To show that coral reefs work with other habitats to support marine life
To prove that coral reefs are more dangerous than forests
To explain why storms always begin near the coast
To compare coral reefs with desert ecosystems

questions.evidence: The final paragraph says scientists study reef health to understand the wider coastal system.
questions.explanation: The examples of seagrass beds and mangroves help show that reefs are part of a connected habitat system.

title: The Development of Public Libraries
passage:
Public libraries began as small collections that were usually available only to people who could pay a fee or join a society. Over time, many communities decided that books and information should be available to everyone, not only to a narrow group. This idea helped public libraries grow into civic institutions supported by local taxes and public policy.

As library systems expanded, they changed in practical ways. Large reading rooms were built for students and researchers, while children's corners, newspaper shelves, and reference desks made the buildings useful to more visitors. Later, libraries added borrowing services, meeting rooms, and public computers. These changes were not made simply to make the buildings look modern. They were responses to the needs of the communities around them.

Today, libraries still lend books, but they also offer language classes, job search help, tax forms, and quiet spaces for study. Their role has grown from storing books to connecting people with information and services. The author mentions job training, resume help, and internet access near the end of the passage to show that public libraries now serve as community centers as well as places for reading.

questions.question: What is the passage mainly about?

options.text: How public libraries developed into broader community institutions
Why private book clubs replaced all libraries
How to build a large reading room from scratch
Why newspapers are more important than books

questions.evidence: The passage explains how libraries changed from limited collections into public community centers.
questions.explanation: The entire text focuses on the growth and changing role of public libraries.

questions.question: What helped public libraries grow into civic institutions?

options.text: A decision to limit books to researchers only
Support from local taxes and public policy
A decline in the number of visitors
A rule that every visitor must pay a fee

questions.evidence: This idea helped public libraries grow into civic institutions supported by local taxes and public policy.
questions.explanation: The passage directly identifies taxes and policy support as important to library growth.

questions.question: In the passage, the word narrow most nearly means

options.text: limited
wide
quiet
public

questions.evidence: Public libraries should be available to everyone, not only to a narrow group.
questions.explanation: The contrast with everyone shows that narrow means restricted or limited.

questions.question: What can be inferred about libraries today?

options.text: They matter less because books are no longer needed
They continue to adapt to the needs of their communities
They only serve children and students
They have stopped offering information services

questions.evidence: Libraries added borrowing services, meeting rooms, public computers, and other services in response to community needs.
questions.explanation: The passage shows that libraries remain useful because they keep changing with the community.

questions.question: Why does the author mention job training, resume help, and internet access near the end?

options.text: To show that libraries now support practical community needs
To explain why reading rooms are no longer needed
To argue that libraries should stop lending books
To prove that taxes are unnecessary for public services

questions.evidence: Their role has grown from storing books to connecting people with information and services.
questions.explanation: The examples show that libraries now do much more than lend books.

title: How Migratory Animals Navigate
passage:
Every year, many migratory animals travel long distances between feeding and breeding areas. Their journeys are impressive not only because of the distance involved, but also because the animals often return to the same general places at about the same time each year. To do this, they use more than one navigation clue.

Birds can track the sun during the day and the stars at night, but they also notice coastlines, mountains, and other landmarks when those features are available. Sea turtles, which spend much of their lives in the ocean, appear to sense the Earth's magnetic field as well as the angle of waves near the shore. Salmon rely on smell when they return from the sea to the streams where they were born. In each case, the animals combine signals rather than trusting only one cue.

Scientists believe this flexible system helps animals stay accurate even when weather, light, or landscape changes. If clouds hide the stars, a bird may still use landmarks. If waves shift, a turtle may depend more on magnetic information. The author includes birds, turtles, and salmon in separate examples to show that different species use different combinations of cues, even though all of them are trying to solve the same problem of navigation.

questions.question: What is the passage mainly about?

options.text: The many reasons animals migrate in groups
The different clues migratory animals use to navigate
Why oceans are warmer than rivers
How scientists measure the weight of birds

questions.evidence: The passage explains that animals use the sun, stars, landmarks, magnetic fields, and smell to find their way.
questions.explanation: The main point is the navigation system migratory animals use, not migration in general.

questions.question: According to the passage, what do salmon use when they return to the streams where they were born?

options.text: The sound of waves
The smell of the water
The shape of the moon
The position of coral reefs

questions.evidence: Salmon rely on smell when they return from the sea to the streams where they were born.
questions.explanation: The passage directly states that smell is the important clue for salmon.

questions.question: In the passage, the word cue most nearly means

options.text: a clue or signal
a food source
a season of rain
a place to hide

questions.evidence: The animals combine signals rather than trusting only one cue.
questions.explanation: The context shows that cue means a sign or clue that helps navigation.

questions.question: What can be inferred if one navigation clue is blocked?

options.text: The animal must stop migrating forever
The animal may use another clue instead
The animal will always get lost immediately
The animal can only move during storms

questions.evidence: The passage says animals combine signals rather than trusting only one cue.
questions.explanation: Because they use several clues, they can still navigate when one clue is unavailable.

questions.question: Why does the author discuss birds, turtles, and salmon in separate examples?

options.text: To show that all animals use the exact same signal
To show that different species use different combinations of cues
To prove that migration only happens in water
To describe the food each animal eats

questions.evidence: The author includes birds, turtles, and salmon in separate examples to show that different species use different combinations of cues.
questions.explanation: The examples are organized to compare several species and the navigation clues they rely on.
